State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 8071, SACRAMENTO, CA [illegible] SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 28, 1983

Mr. Irish D. Mitchell
402 E. 6th Street
Beaumont, CA 92223

RE: Your Request For Advice, Our
Advice No. A-83-097

Dear Mr. Mitchell:

Thank you for your request for advice on the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act. During your duties,
under the Act are not in question. I cannot provide you with
specific advice. (Government Code Section 83114.) However, I
can provide you with a general explanation of the law.

The facts, as presented to me, are that on April 15, 1983,
the City of Beaumont held a special election on an issue
affecting the redevelopment plan. The city council provided the
chamber of commerce with $1,700 for a "Yes" campaign to be spent
on a mass mailing, signs, and other political activity in
support of the ballot measure. The Chamber of Commerce formed a
committee and expended these funds in accordance with this
agreement. You are a citizen who opposed the ballot measure and
your question is whether the allocation was a proper use of city
funds.

The provisions of the Political Reform Act do not cover the
propriety of the City's use of the money. The Act only covers
the campaign reporting requirements. Under the Act, an entity
which receives $500 or more in contributions is a recipient
committee under Government Code Section 82013(a).1/ A
recipient committee must report all of the contributions that it
receives on Form 428, Schedule A.2/

_______________________
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

2/ A recipient committee must also report all other
receipts and expenditures.

[illegible], if it is
[illegible] aid in
[illegible]

[illegible] purposes. This letter is to inform you that as a courtesy to a
contributor to a candidate, I want to apprise you of a measure it full and complete [illegible] in
the donor in said [illegible] (as modifier [illegible]).

An entity which makes [illegible] from § [illegible] contributions, and
which does not receive any contributions in a given day, does have a filing
requirement under the Act.

If, after reviewing the campaign statements of the Chamber
of Commerce's committee, you do not find that they are timely
completed, you may file a complaint with the Fair Political
Practices Commission. With regard to the question of whether
the City properly used its "red tax" under to influence the
election, you should contact the Attorney General's Office or
your local District Attorney. If you believe that the funds
were improperly used, you can file a complaint with either of
these agencies.

If I can be of any further help to you on the provisions of
the Political Reform Act, please feel free to contact me at
416/322-9901.

Very truly yours,

James Shanke McLean
JAMES SHANKE McLEAN
Political Reform Division

If, after reviewing the campaign [illegible] at the Chamber of Commerce's condition, you do not find that they are properly completed, you may file a complaint with the Fair Political Practices Commission with regard to the misuse or misappropriation of the city property. If it "tied" Max" under to balance the election, you should contact the Attorney General's Office or your local District Attorney. If you believe that the funds were improperly used, you can file a complaint with either [illegible] agencies.

In light of the foregoing [illegible] to [illegible] to [illegible] provisions of the Political Reform [illegible] [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO 95804 • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO 95814

April 28, 1983

Bob Poescal, Chairman
Beaumont Chamber of Commerce
450 E. Fourth Street
Beaumont, CA 92223

Dear Mr. Poescal:

I recently received a request for advice on a question
involving the Chamber of Commerce. For your information, I have
included a copy of my response.

If, after reviewing my letter, you would like additional
advice, please feel free to contact me at 916-322-5901.

Very truly yours,

James Shank McLean
James Shank McLean
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question: Whether the allocation of $2,700 in 'bed tax' revenue by the City of Beaumont to the Chamber of Commerce for political activity in support of a ballot measure was a proper use of city funds.
sections.conclusion: The provisions of the Political Reform Act do not cover the propriety of the City's use of the money. The Act only covers the campaign reporting requirements. Under the Act, an entity which receives $500 or more in contributions is a recipient committee and must report contributions on Form 420, Schedule A. An entity making less than $1,000 in contributions does not have a filing requirement. For questions about improper use of city funds, the requester should contact the Attorney General's Office or local District Attorney.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asked whether a city's allocation of bed tax revenue to the Chamber of Commerce for campaign spending supporting a ballot measure complied with the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the Political Reform Act does not address whether the city properly used its bed tax funds, only campaign reporting requirements. The requester should contact the Attorney General or District Attorney regarding the propriety of the city's expenditure.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters (some appear to be templates or duplicates with inconsistent dates/recipients). Primary substantive response is the first letter dated April 28, 1983 from Janis Shank McLean to Irish D. Mitchell regarding Advice No. A-83-097. Subsequent letters appear to be notice copies to interested parties. Some text quality issues in latter portions suggest OCR or transcription errors.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the allocation of $2,700 in 'bed tax' revenue by the City of Beaumont to the Chamber of Commerce for political activity in support of a ballot measure was a proper use of city funds.

CONCLUSION: The provisions of the Political Reform Act do not cover the propriety of the City's use of the money. The Act only covers the campaign reporting requirements. Under the Act, an entity which receives $500 or more in contributions is a recipient committee and must report contributions on Form 420, Schedule A. An entity making less than $1,000 in contributions does not have a filing requirement. For questions about improper use of city funds, the requester should contact the Attorney General's Office or local District Attorney.
embedding.first_500_words: April 28, 1983 Mr. Irish D. Mitchell 402 S. 6th Street Beaumont, CA 92222 Re: Your Request For Advice, Our Advice No. A-83-097 Dear Mr. Mitchell: Thank you for your request for advice on the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act. Because your duties under the Act are not in question, I cannot provide you with specific advice. (Government Code Section 8114.) However, I can provide you with a general explanation of the law. The facts, as presented to me, are that on April 15, 1983, the City of Beaumont held a special election on an issue affecting the redevelopment plan. The City Council provided the Chamber of Commerce with $2,700 in "bed tax" revenue to be spent on a mass mailing, signs, and other political activity in support of the ballot measure. The Chamber of Commerce formed a committee and expended these funds in accordance with this agreement. You are a citizen who opposed the ballot measure and your question is whether the allocation was a proper use of city funds. The provisions of the Political Reform Act do not cover the propriety of the City's use of the money. The Act only covers the campaign reporting requirements. Under the Act, an entity which receives $500 or more in contributions is a recipient committee under Government Code Section 82013(a).1/ A recipient committee must report all of the contributions that it receives on Form 420, Schedule A.2/ 1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 2/ A recipient committee must also report all other receipts and expenditures. Mr. H. M. Donahue April 26, 1984 Page 1/2 A "contributor" is defined as an entity which makes contributions for political purposes. These entities must file reports, and which are made to a committee for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters or the legislature in the passage of a measure if full and adequate disclosure notice is not received by the donor (2 Cal. Com. Code Sections 10100-10105). An entity which makes less than $1,000 in contributions, and which does not receive any contributions, does not have a filing requirement under the Act. If, after reviewing the campaign statements of the Chamber of Commerce's committee, you do not feel that they are properly completed, you may file a complaint with the Fair Political Practices Commission. With regard to the question of whether the City properly used its "Bed Tax" funds to influence the election, you should contact the Attorney General's Office or your local District Attorney. If you believe that the funds were improperly used, you can file complaints with either of these agencies. If I can be of any further help to you on the provisions of the Political Reform Act, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5901. Very truly yours, Janis Shank McLean Janis Shank McLean Assistant, Legal Division JSM:km cc: Mr. H. M. Donahue Bob Poeschel Ann Conners April 26, 1974 Mr. William J. Smith City
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter A-83-097 responding to an inquiry about whether a city's allocation of bed tax revenue to a chamber of commerce for campaign activity supporting a ballot measure complied with the Political Reform Act. The FPPC clarified that the Act covers only campaign reporting requirements, not the propriety of public fund expenditures, and directed the requester to other agencies for that question.
